title: Performance Optimization
objectives: Understand Android performance via a real-life analogy
Hit the 16 ms frame budget (60fps) reliably
Diagnose jank, ANRs, and memory leaks

sections.markdown:
**Think of a train's tight timetable.**

- The Android display refreshes 60 times per second (some devices 90/120 Hz). That gives your app exactly **16.67 ms per frame** to prepare the next screen. Miss the deadline and the same frame shows twice → the user sees **jank** (a stutter, hitch).
- **The main thread is the ONE train on the ONE track** delivering frames to the display. Anything you put on that track — network calls, big JSONs, huge image decodes, sync file I/O — makes the train late. Late trains cause pile-ups (frame drops).
- **Off-main-thread work is a parallel goods track**: coroutines / WorkManager / IO dispatcher. Anything longer than a few ms should ride the goods track.
- **Memory leaks** are like train cars that never get uncoupled — they pile up in the yard until the whole system runs out of siding space (OutOfMemory). Every held-onto Activity is one such stuck car.
- **App startup** is the initial 'get the train out of the depot' — every millisecond you spend in `Application.onCreate` and `Activity.onCreate` before the first frame delays the whole ride.

**Performance is timetable discipline.** Measure with the Profiler, cut what's late.

**Android performance** is the combined discipline of hitting the display frame budget (16 ms at 60fps), starting fast, using memory efficiently, and shipping a small binary. The main levers: **thread hygiene** (nothing slow on the UI thread), **memory hygiene** (no leaks, right-sized bitmaps), **rendering hygiene** (avoid overdraw, keep view hierarchies flat), and **startup hygiene** (defer everything you can).
## The four pillars of Android performance

rows: Work on main thread; deep view hierarchies; overdraw
Move to coroutine; flatten layouts; profile with GPU Rendering; use Compose stability keys
Leaked Activities/Views; over-sized bitmaps; unbounded caches
LeakCanary; Glide/Coil; LruCache; use `applicationContext` for long-lived deps
App Startup library; Baseline Profiles; lazy init; Macrobenchmark measurement
Frequent wakeups; unnecessary polling; large payloads
WorkManager batching; Firebase Cloud Messaging push; gzip; efficient formats (Protobuf)

sections.markdown: ## The essential toolset

rows: CPU, memory, network, energy — live view of your app
Memory leaks with the full retain chain — a must-have in dev builds
Startup + jank + frame-timing measured on a real device, scriptable
AOT-compile hot code paths for faster startup + smoother scrolling
Rendering histogram — how many frames dropped
System-level trace including binder, GC, and kernel scheduling
Fails builds on main-thread disk/network access

sections.markdown: **The interview one-liner:** 'measure before you optimize.' Junior devs guess ("maybe HashMap is slow?") and rewrite the wrong thing. Senior devs profile the app first, find the actual bottleneck (usually main-thread I/O or bitmap decoding), and fix that single thing.
## Sample program — fixing jank, a leak, and startup
## Rendering — how to hit 60 fps
**1. Keep the main thread free.** Every ms counts against your 16 ms budget. Network, DB, disk, JSON parsing → off main.
**2. Flatten view hierarchies.** Deep nesting slows measure/layout passes. Use `ConstraintLayout` instead of nested `LinearLayout`s. In Compose, avoid unnecessary `Box`/`Column` wrappers.
**3. Fix overdraw.** Each pixel drawn multiple times = wasted GPU. Enable **Show Overdraw** in Developer Options; aim for mostly clear/blue (1-2x).
**4. Use RecyclerView correctly.** `ListAdapter` with `DiffUtil`; `setHasFixedSize(true)`; `setItemViewCacheSize` for smooth scrolling; view types for heterogeneous lists.
**5. Compose stability + keys.** Use stable data classes; add `key(id)` in `items(...)`; mark unstable types `@Immutable`/`@Stable`; use `derivedStateOf` for computed state.
## Memory — the leak-and-image dance
**1. LeakCanary in dev builds.** Automatic leak detection with the retain chain. Fix leaks as they surface.
**2. Use `applicationContext` for long-lived references.** Never store an Activity Context in a singleton.
**3. Bounded caches.** Use `LruCache` / `DiskLruCache`. Never let a Map grow forever.
**4. Downsample images.** Use `Glide`/`Coil` — they resize on the fly to the target ImageView size. Never load a 4000×3000 photo into a 200×200 thumbnail.
**5. Null ViewBinding in `onDestroyView`.** Standard fragment idiom.
## Startup — the first-impression battle
**1. Measure with Macrobenchmark.** Cold-start / warm-start / hot-start timing on a real device. Set a budget (say, < 1 s cold on mid-tier).
**2. Cut work from `Application.onCreate`.** Non-critical init → post to Handler or a background dispatcher.
**3. Adopt Baseline Profiles.** Ship pre-computed AOT-compile hints; measurable improvement in startup and scroll (20–40%).
**4. Use the App Startup library.** Central place to declare initializers with priorities and dependencies.
**5. Show a fast splash / first paint.** Android 12+ splash-screen API renders a system splash immediately; make sure your first frame doesn't fetch anything sync.
## Common beginner mistakes
**Mistake 1: Optimizing without profiling.** 'HashMap must be slow' → 3 days rewriting map code → real bottleneck was 400 ms of JSON parsing on the main thread.
**Mistake 2: Ignoring the `<application>` `largeHeap="true"` shortcut.** It doesn't fix leaks; it delays OOM by minutes. Fix the leaks.
**Mistake 3: `parallelStream()` / creating threads directly.** Threads are expensive; use coroutines with proper dispatchers.
**Mistake 4: R8 disabled in release builds.** Ships un-minified bytecode; apps 30–50% larger, slower on-device parse, less confidential.
**Mistake 5: Debug builds when benchmarking.** Always benchmark release builds — R8, no Compose Live Edit, no logs.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Enable Show Overdraw** on Developer Options and hunt for red areas in your app; flatten one layout to reduce overdraw.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — Integrate LeakCanary** and intentionally leak an Activity (Activity ref in a singleton). Fix it.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — Move a slow onCreate call off main.** Wrap with `lifecycleScope.launch { withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { … } }`.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Macrobenchmark cold-start** and try to cut 20% by deferring init in `Application.onCreate`.
**Practice 5 (Expert) — Generate + apply Baseline Profile** and remeasure. Document the delta.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What causes jank on Android and how do you fix it?**

**A:** **Jank** is when a frame takes longer than 16 ms to prepare, so the display shows the previous frame again — the user sees a stutter. Causes: (1) **Main-thread work** — network, DB, big JSON parsing, image decoding on the UI thread. (2) **Deep view hierarchies** — measure/layout costs balloon with nested containers. (3) **Overdraw** — the GPU redrawing pixels multiple times per frame. (4) **Bad RecyclerView usage** — no DiffUtil, no view recycling, layout thrash. **Fix:** move work off main (coroutine + `Dispatchers.IO`/`Default`), flatten to `ConstraintLayout`, enable Show Overdraw and remove unnecessary backgrounds, use `ListAdapter + DiffUtil`. **Measure with:** Systrace/Perfetto, GPU Rendering profiler, Macrobenchmark's frame-timing metrics.

**Q2: How do memory leaks happen on Android?**

**A:** A leak = an object cannot be garbage-collected because something outside it holds a reference. Classic causes: **(1) Activity/Context in a singleton** — the singleton outlives the Activity, so GC can't reclaim the Activity's ~1 MB. Rotation multiplies the leak. **(2) Long-lived callback / listener / handler** referencing `this` outer Activity. **(3) A coroutine launched in `GlobalScope`** referencing UI. **(4) Static field holding a View**. **(5) Anonymous inner classes / lambdas** holding implicit `this`. **Fixes:** never store Activity/Context in singletons — use `applicationContext` or DI; scope coroutines to `viewModelScope`/`lifecycleScope`; use `WeakReference` if you must hold a Context. **Detect with LeakCanary** — it surfaces the exact retain chain automatically.

**Q3: How do you handle large images to avoid OOM?**

**A:** Never load a full-size image into memory. Use a proper image library — **Glide** or **Coil** (Kotlin-first, coroutine-native). They: (1) decode to the target `ImageView` size — a 4000×3000 photo becomes 200×200. (2) Use `RGB_565` or `HARDWARE` `Bitmap.Config` when appropriate. (3) Cache to memory (LruCache) and disk automatically. (4) Cancel in-flight requests when the view is recycled. (5) Handle lifecycle so requests cancel on Activity destroy. For **very large images** (whole-screen photo viewers), consider `SubsamplingScaleImageView` (tile-based) or Android's `BitmapRegionDecoder`. **Rule:** never do raw `BitmapFactory.decodeStream(...)` in production.

**Q4: What tools do you use for Android profiling?**

**A:** **Android Studio Profiler** (CPU/memory/network/energy — the daily driver). **Systrace / Perfetto** for system-level traces including binder and GC. **LeakCanary** for memory leaks with retain chains. **Macrobenchmark** for scripted startup + frame-timing measurements on a real device. **Baseline Profiles** to AOT-compile hot paths for the release build. **`adb shell dumpsys gfxinfo <pkg> framestats`** for a rendering histogram. **StrictMode** to fail builds when main-thread I/O sneaks in. Any experienced Android dev uses at least the Profiler + LeakCanary + Macrobenchmark regularly.

**Q5: How do you improve app startup time?**

**A:** Three levers. **(1) Cut `Application.onCreate` work.** Everything you do here runs before the first frame. Move non-critical init (analytics warm-up, image cache warm-up) to a `Handler.post`, a coroutine, or WorkManager. **(2) Adopt the App Startup library** — declares initializers with priorities and dependencies; one entry point instead of Content Providers scattered by third-party SDKs. **(3) Ship a Baseline Profile.** Pre-compiled hot paths cut cold-start by ~30% on typical apps. **Measure with Macrobenchmark cold/warm/hot** — you can't improve what you don't measure. **Also:** use the Android 12+ splash-screen API so the user sees something instant, even while your first frame is loading.

**Q6: What is Baseline Profile?**

**A:** A pre-defined list of method signatures that the Android Runtime AOT-compiles ahead of first use. Normally ART compiles methods JIT — hot methods get compiled after N executions, causing early-launch jank while things warm up. A Baseline Profile ships the 'critical path' ahead of time so key methods are already fast on first launch. Google ships one for `androidx.compose.*` automatically. You can generate one for YOUR app by running Macrobenchmark's baseline-profile generator with your critical user journey. Typical improvement: 20–40% cold-start, noticeably smoother first scroll.

**Q7: What is StrictMode?**

**A:** A developer-mode monitor that logs (and can crash the app) whenever your code performs disk or network I/O on the main thread, or leaks Activities/SQLite cursors. Enable in `Application.onCreate` for debug builds:
```kotlin
StrictMode.setThreadPolicy(StrictMode.ThreadPolicy.Builder().detectAll().penaltyLog().build())
StrictMode.setVmPolicy(StrictMode.VmPolicy.Builder().detectAll().penaltyLog().build())
```
Big teams enable `penaltyDeath()` so violations crash in dev — impossible to accidentally merge main-thread I/O. Doesn't run in release, so no user impact. It's one of the cheapest wins for Android performance discipline.

**Q8: How do you reduce app size (APK/AAB)?**

**A:** **(1) Ship an AAB (Android App Bundle), not APK.** Play delivers device-specific slices — often halving install size. **(2) Enable R8** (default in release). Removes unused code + resources, minifies. **(3) Enable resource shrinking.** Removes unused drawables, strings, layouts. **(4) Use vector drawables** for icons, not PNG variants. **(5) Compress PNGs / prefer WebP** for photos. **(6) Split assets with Play Feature Delivery** — only download on-demand. **(7) Remove unused libraries** — check with the APK Analyzer in Studio. Big wins are usually 20–50% size reduction with these alone.

## Real-world usage

- **All FAANG Android apps** — heavy investment in performance: Baseline Profiles, R8, Macrobenchmark, custom traces.
- **Uber/Lyft** — startup obsessive; every ms of first-map-frame matters for conversion.
- **Instagram/Snapchat** — memory obsessive; camera + image processing is the biggest OOM risk.
- **Netflix** — jank obsessive; a stuttering video-player list would tank engagement.
- **Media apps (Spotify, Podcasts)** — foreground-service correctness, low idle battery.
- **Enterprise apps** — cold-start battle to keep users; teams routinely target < 1.5 s cold on mid-tier phones.

## Advanced notes

- **`Choreographer.postFrameCallback`** — measure frame timing yourself.
- **`ANR-Watchdog` library** — detects main-thread hangs in production.
- **Firebase Perf Monitoring** — production traces + custom metrics.
- **JankStats** (Jetpack) — measure jank in the wild.
- **`android.view.Choreographer.FrameCallback`** — hook into the vsync loop.
- **`InProcessTest`** and **`androidx.tracing`** — annotate custom trace sections.
- **Compose stability inference** — `@Immutable`, `@Stable`, KSP-generated stability info in newer versions.
- **App size and startup are tracked in Play Console vitals** — Google downranks apps with poor vitals in Play Search.

flashcards.back: Keep main thread free — move network/DB/parsing off.
Activity/Context stored in a singleton — GC can't reclaim it.
Glide or Coil — downsample to view size automatically.
LeakCanary in debug builds — surfaces the retain chain.
Defer non-critical init; use App Startup library; ship a Baseline Profile.
Macrobenchmark cold/warm/hot on a real device.

options: Activity Context held in a singleton

challenges.text: Enable StrictMode in debug; fix any main-thread I/O it reveals.
Add LeakCanary; intentionally leak an Activity; fix it.
Flatten a nested LinearLayout into ConstraintLayout; measure the perf delta.
Replace manual Bitmap decoding with Glide/Coil.
Run Macrobenchmark cold-start; cut 20% by deferring init.
Generate a Baseline Profile for your critical journey; ship it; remeasure.
Full audit: Overdraw, LayoutInspector, LeakCanary, Macrobenchmark, dumpsys gfxinfo; publish before/after metrics.

interviewQuestions: What causes jank on Android and how do you fix it?
How do memory leaks happen on Android?
How do you handle large images to avoid OOM?
What tools do you use for Android profiling?
How do you improve app startup time?
How do you reduce app size (APK/AAB)?
